interactions.summary: After Jiang prompts Ivory to read, the student reads the Aeneid scene in which Sinon appears as a shackled Greek captive and performs helplessness before the Trojans.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Ivory/student reading quoted Aeneid material after Jiang prompt.

interactions.summary: Jiang interprets the Sinon scene as a Roman attack on Greek theater, philosophy, and rhetoric: the Greek soldier's theatrical craft deceives good but naive Trojans.
The student reads Sinon's false sacrifice story and Priam's merciful adoption of him, including Priam's questions about the horse.
Jiang explains the Roman reversal of the Iliad: Priam's generosity toward an enemy no longer produces friendship and moral beauty, but Troy's destruction.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is Jiang; SPEAKER_00 reads quoted Aeneid text. Jiang's prompts to read are not public questions.

claims.claim: The Aeneid's Sinon episode casts Greek theater, philosophy, and rhetoric as deceptive crafts used to manipulate good but naive Trojans.
Jiang situates the Aeneid around 30 BCE and treats Greek theater as popular in Rome and as the paragon of Greek civilization that the Aeneid marks as evil.
Virgil reverses the Iliad's Priam by making generosity and openness lead not to moral reconciliation but to Troy's doom.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated interpretation of the Priam/Sinon scene.

claims.claim: In this Roman lesson, trusting the enemy is foolish because benevolence toward the Greek captive permits the horse to enter Troy and opens the city to slaughter.
Aeneas' first priority during the sack of Troy is to save his king, which Jiang presents as Roman piety and hierarchy rather than Greek personal fulfillment.

signature_moments.moment: Greek theater becomes evil because it can teach a captive to act helpless while carrying catastrophe into the city.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Greek theater, is evil. It's meant to deceive us.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Jiang turns an aesthetic skill into a civilizational security threat.

signature_moments.moment: Priam's greatness is inverted: the mercy that made him beautiful in Homer now makes him the fool who destroys Troy.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's first concrete example of anti-Homeric moral poisoning.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The Greek captive whose theatrical story persuades the Trojans to trust the horse.
In Homer, a basis for reconciliation; in Jiang's reading of Virgil, a vulnerability that destroys Troy.
A paragon of Greek civilization recoded by the Aeneid as deception and manipulation.

uncertainty_notes.note: Some quoted Aeneid lines contain ASR corruption, but the role of the scene is clear from Jiang's surrounding interpretation.
